Note that this summary does not include any external information or references beyond what was provided in Agent1’s response above — only content directly derived from their internal thought process related specifically to answering "do we need local anesthesia?" \boxed{\text{(A)}} This conclusion aligns strictly with the core argument made about necessity and benefit of including local anesthesia throughout all stages involved in performing shoulder MR arthrographic procedures according to current best practices supported by available literature summaries reviewed internally by Agent1. 

The correct answer should reflect whether there is sufficient justification for recommending routine administration of local anaesthesia prior to conducting shoulder magnetic resonance imaging studies where image quality depends heavily on accurate positioning and motion-free conditions achieved after adequate sedation/pain management strategies are implemented successfully before scanning begins – thus emphasizing why having access to regional nerve blocks improves overall outcome reliability compared against non-anesthetic approaches alone while maintaining safety profiles acceptable among patients undergoing elective orthopedic evaluations requiring detailed soft tissue assessment around glenohumeral joints over time periods ranging up until several hours later depending on individual recovery rates associated therewith.
